description: SRE Four Golden Signals — Latency · Traffic · Errors · Saturation — mapped to the enterprise reliability benchmark: availability, performance, traffic variability, recovery velocity, data integrity, and security. Supplementary: Continuous Profiling via Pyroscope.

options.content:
## GatewayZ — AI Inference Gateway

| Signal | SLI Target |
|--------|------------|
| ⚡ **Latency** | P50 < 1 s · **P90 < 2 s** · P95 < 3 s |
| 🚦 **Traffic** | Baseline ± 3× (TrafficSpike alert) |
| 🚨 **Errors** | < 0.5% error rate — 99.5% availability SLO |
| 📊 **Saturation** | CPU < 80% · 429 rate < 10% |

**Error Budget**: 0.5% / month · ~21.9 min allowed downtime  
_Reliability: SLI/SLO-driven · Error budget burn alerting · Multi-layer detection (Metrics · Logs · Traces · Anomaly) · Recovery tracking (MTTR · restart velocity)_

panels.title: P95 (5xx)
panels.description: P95 of only failed (5xx) requests. A low value here means errors are failing fast (good). A high value means errors are also slow — double punishment for your users.

panels.title: Latency Percentiles Over Time — P50 · P90 · P95 · P99
panels.description: Dashed red line shows the P95 of failed (5xx) requests. If this line spikes independently of the success lines, errors are triggering immediately and poisoning your overall latency average — exactly why we never trust the mean.

panels.title: RPS
panels.description: Total inbound requests per second across all endpoints and HTTP methods.

panels.title: Requests (5m)
panels.description: Absolute count of requests received in the last 5-minute window. Use alongside RPS for traffic shape analysis.

panels.title: Success RPS
panels.description: RPS for only non-5xx responses. Compare with total RPS — a large gap means errors are eating into real capacity.

panels.title: Bandwidth In
panels.description: Data volume flowing into the service per second. Critical for data-heavy workloads (LLM token streams). Requires request_size instrumentation to be enabled on the backend.

panels.title: HTTP Request Rate by Status Class
panels.description: Traffic segmented by HTTP status class. A spike in total RPS with no 5xx growth = capacity problem. Normal RPS with growing 5xx = code problem.

panels.title: Bandwidth — Inbound & Outbound (bytes/sec)
panels.description: Critical for LLM token streaming workloads. Shows if you are approaching network saturation. Will read 0 if request/response size instrumentation is not enabled on the FastAPI backend.

panels.title: Error Rate %
panels.description: The primary SRE correctness signal. Green < 1%, Yellow 1-5%, Red > 5%. This is the 'rate' in your error budget burn.

panels.title: 5xx Rate/s
panels.description: Server-side failures: connection timeouts, unhandled exceptions, upstream provider failures. These are your loudest, most actionable errors.

panels.title: SLO Breach %
panels.description: Requests that completed successfully (200 OK) but exceeded the 2-second SLO threshold. A 10-second '200 OK' is still a functional failure from the user's perspective.

panels.title: 4xx Rate/s
panels.description: Client-initiated errors (auth failures, bad input, rate limiting). High 4xx rate can indicate abuse, broken clients, or API contract changes.

panels.title: Error Rate by Type — Explicit · Policy Breach · Client
panels.description: Three error categories: Explicit (server failures), Policy (slow 200s violating SLO), Client (bad requests). Separating these avoids alert fatigue — 4xx spikes are usually abuse, not outages.

panels.title: Errors by HTTP Status Code (4xx & 5xx stacked)
panels.description: Stacked bar breakdown of all error status codes. Useful for identifying if a specific status (e.g. 503, 429) is dominating. Excludes 2xx/3xx success codes.

panels.title: Exceptions/s
panels.description: Rate of unhandled exceptions reported via the FastAPI prometheus instrumentation. The first detection layer. Any non-zero value indicates application-level errors.

panels.title: Log ERRORs/s
panels.description: Rate of log lines containing 'ERROR' from the GatewayZ application logs via Loki. Captures stack traces, exception messages, and unstructured error events. Complements metric-based detection.

panels.title: Trace Fails/s
panels.description: Rate of failed spans from Tempo's service graph (downstream dependency failures). Detects errors in external calls — provider APIs, DB, cache — that may not surface in application metrics.

panels.title: Error Anomaly (× base)
panels.description: Current error rate as a multiple of the 1h baseline. 1x = normal. 2x = double normal error rate. 5x+ = anomaly. Detects spikes that are statistically abnormal even when absolute error rate is low.

panels.title: Multi-Layer Error Signal Correlation  |  Metrics · Logs · Traces
panels.description: Overlays error signals from all three observable layers on a single timeline. Signal correlation (two or more layers spiking together) confirms a real error event and speeds up root-cause triage. A spike on Traces but not Metrics = downstream dependency. A spike on Logs but not Metrics = unhandled exception without HTTP error code.

panels.title: Data-Level Anomaly — Error Rate vs Baseline
panels.description: Current error rate (%) against 1h and 24h rolling baselines computed by recording rules. A current line rising above both baselines indicates a statistically abnormal error event even if the absolute value appears small. Early detection at low absolute error rates is critical for maintaining the 99.5% SLO.

panels.title: 🗺️  Service Graph & Topology — Upstream vs Downstream Failure Analysis  |  Powered by Tempo metrics-generator → Mimir

options.content:
### Service Graph — what it shows and why the panels may read "No data"

This section reads from `traces_service_graph_*` metrics that Tempo's `metrics_generator` writes to Mimir. **These metrics only exist when Tempo receives a trace that contains spans from at least two different `service.name` values.** GatewayZ is a single-service gateway — the AI providers it calls (OpenAI, Anthropic, OpenRouter, etc.) are external HTTP endpoints that do not participate in OTel tracing and do not propagate `traceparent` headers back. Every trace Tempo receives therefore has only one `service.name`, so the service-graphs processor has no edges to draw.

**What would make data appear here:** If the backend adds a child span with `peer.service = "openai"` (or the provider name) for each outbound call, Tempo will generate `gatewayz-backend → openai` edges. That is the accurate framing — not "Service A vs Service B" in a microservices sense, but **"gateway processing time vs provider inference time"** on a per-request basis.

**Cross-signal navigation (works today):** Click the blue ◆ exemplar dot on any latency graph to jump to the exact Tempo trace. In Loki log panels click **"View Trace"** next to any `trace_id` to open the Tempo waterfall. In Tempo, click any span → **"Related metrics"** links to Mimir, **"Related logs"** links to Loki.

panels.title: Live Service Dependency Map
panels.description: Auto-generated from Tempo span data. Node size = request volume. Edge color = error rate (green→yellow→red). Click a node to filter Tempo traces to that service. Requires Tempo metrics_generator service-graphs processor (already enabled).

panels.title: Service-to-Service Request Rate (RPS)
panels.description: Traffic volume on each service edge observed by Tempo. A sudden drop to zero on an edge means that dependency is completely unreachable — a leading indicator of a cascading failure.

targets.expr: sum by(client, server) (rate(traces_service_graph_request_failed_total[$__rate_interval])) / sum by(client, server) (rate(traces_service_graph_request_total[$__rate_interval]))

panels.title: Service-to-Service Error Rate %
panels.description: Cross-service error rate per edge. This tells you if a latency spike in the gateway is coming from an upstream AI provider failure (server edge goes red) or from gateway-internal code (client edge stays green).

panels.title: Cross-Service Latency — P95 & P99 per Edge
panels.description: P95/P99 latency for every client→server pair in the service graph. Separates upstream provider slowness from gateway processing time — the key to distinguishing capacity problems from code problems.

panels.title: 🎯  SLO Compliance & Error Budget  |  99.5% Success Rate · Error Budget Burn Rate Tracking
SLO Compliance — Success Rate % (window)
panels.description: % of requests that returned non-5xx. SLO target: 99.5%. Uses Mimir for long-range queries.

panels.title: Error Budget Remaining %
panels.description: How much error budget is left. 0% = SLO breached. 100% = no errors used yet.

panels.title: Burn Rate — 1h  (page if > 14.4×)
panels.description: Error burn rate relative to SLO error budget. >14.4× means you'll exhaust the 30-day budget in ~2h. >6× means ~5h.

panels.title: Burn Rate — 6h  (page if > 6×)
panels.description: Slower burn rate. >6× sustained for 6h will exhaust the 30-day budget in ~5 days.

panels.title: Cache Hit Rate % — Data Integrity Signal
panels.description: Low cache hit rate means more DB reads, risking stale or inconsistent data. A sudden drop is a data integrity and performance warning.

panels.title: Error Budget Burn Rate Over Time  (1h · 6h windows)
panels.description: Values above the threshold lines indicate fast error budget consumption. Burn Rate 1 = consuming at exactly SLO-defined pace.

panels.title: CPU %
panels.description: FastAPI/uvicorn CPU usage. Sustained >80% causes event loop lag and tail latency blowup.

panels.title: In-Flight
panels.description: Requests currently being processed. Sustained high values indicate back-pressure or slow providers.

panels.title: File Descriptors %
panels.description: File descriptor exhaustion causes 'too many open files' errors and connection failures. >90% = imminent danger.

panels.title: Uptime
panels.description: Continuous uptime since last restart. A drop to zero followed by recovery shows an unplanned restart event. Primary MTTR proxy.

panels.title: Rate Limit %
panels.description: % of requests being rate-limited. >10% indicates capacity pressure or abuse pattern. Both a saturation and security signal.

panels.title: Auth Failures/s
panels.description: Auth failure rate per second. A spike indicates credential stuffing, key compromise, or misconfigured clients. Security reliability requirement.

panels.title: 🔥  CPU Flamegraph — What’s Hot Right Now
panels.description: Live CPU flamegraph for the current time window. Immediately shows which function/call-stack is consuming the most CPU. Use this the moment a saturation stat turns red.

panels.title: 📊  CPU Load by Endpoint — Saturation Source
panels.description: Which API endpoint is responsible for the saturation? High CPU share on one endpoint points directly to the traffic causing the pressure. Cross-reference with the RPS stat above.
Tenants whose 5-min error rate exceeds 0.5% — the SLO error-budget boundary.

targets.expr: count(  sum by (customer_id)(rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{http_status_code=~"5..",customer_id!=""}[5m]))  / sum by (customer_id)(rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{customer_id!=""}[5m]))  > 0.005) or vector(0)

panels.title: Tenants Breaching SLO

panels.description: Tenants whose P95 response latency has exceeded the 2-second SLO threshold.

targets.expr: count(  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le,customer_id)(    rate(traces_spanmetrics_latency_bucket{customer_id!=""}[5m])  )) > 2) or vector(0)

panels.title: Tenants P95 > 2 s

panels.description: Distinct tenants with at least one request in the last 5 minutes.
panels.title: Active Tenants (5 m)

panels.description: Global 5xx error rate across all tenants. A spike here = system-level failure.
panels.title: System-Wide Error Rate

panels.description: Each row = one tenant.  Colour = error rate: green < 0.5%  |  yellow 0.5–1%  |  orange 1–5%  |  red > 5%.  A sustained red/orange band = active SLO breach.  Pinpoints which tenant is affected and exactly when it started — no other dashboard needed for initial incident triage.

targets.expr: sum by (customer_id)(  rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{    http_status_code=~"5..",customer_id!=""  }[$__rate_interval])) / sum by (customer_id)(  rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{customer_id!=""}[$__rate_interval])) * 100

panels.title: 🗺️  SLO Status by Tenant — Error Rate % Over Time  (red band = active breach)
📡  Tenant Breach Detection  |  Multi-Tenant SLO Health  (expand for state timeline)

panels.description: Tenants whose 5-min error rate exceeds 0.5% — the SLO error-budget boundary.

targets.expr: count(  sum by (customer_id)(rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{http_status_code=~"5..",customer_id!=""}[5m]))  / sum by (customer_id)(rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{customer_id!=""}[5m]))  > 0.005) or vector(0)

panels.title: Tenants Breaching SLO

panels.description: Tenants whose P95 response latency has exceeded the 2-second SLO threshold.

targets.expr: count(  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le,customer_id)(    rate(traces_spanmetrics_latency_bucket{customer_id!=""}[5m])  )) > 2) or vector(0)

panels.title: Tenants P95 > 2 s

panels.description: Distinct tenants with at least one request in the last 5 minutes.
panels.title: Active Tenants (5 m)

panels.description: Global 5xx error rate across all tenants. A spike here = system-level failure.
panels.title: System-Wide Error Rate

panels.description: Each row = one tenant.  Colour = error rate: green < 0.5%  |  yellow 0.5–1%  |  orange 1–5%  |  red > 5%.  A sustained red/orange band = active SLO breach.  Pinpoints which tenant is affected and exactly when it started — no other dashboard needed for initial incident triage.

targets.expr: sum by (customer_id)(  rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{    http_status_code=~"5..",customer_id!=""  }[$__rate_interval])) / sum by (customer_id)(  rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{customer_id!=""}[$__rate_interval])) * 100

panels.title: 🗺️  SLO Status by Tenant — Error Rate % Over Time  (red band = active breach)
🔄  Recovery & Resilience  |  Restart Velocity · MTTR Proxy · Error Budget Velocity
Process Restarts (last 1h)
panels.description: 0 = Stable. Non-zero = unplanned restart occurred. Repeated restarts signal an unresolved root cause requiring investigation.

panels.title: Time Since Last Restart (MTTR Proxy)
panels.description: Green = stable > 1h. Low value just after an incident confirms recovery has started. The rate of increase shows recovery velocity.

panels.title: Recovery Velocity — Restart History & Uptime Over Time
panels.description: Spikes in restart events mark incidents. Rising uptime after a spike shows recovery. Flat, rising uptime = stable system.

panels.title: Error Budget Velocity — Burn Rate (Recovery Context)
panels.description: During recovery, burn rate should fall back toward 1x. Above 14.4x = critical SLO burn; still in incident.

options.content:
## 🔬  Pyroscope — Continuous CPU Profiling

This section ties the profiling layer directly to each of the four golden signal pillars above.

| Golden Signal | What Pyroscope answers |
|---|---|
| **⚡ Latency** | *Which Python functions are burning CPU during slow requests?* Flamegraph filtered by `endpoint` reveals the hot path — provider HTTP call? token chunking? Redis rate-limit? |
| **🚦 Traffic** | *Which endpoints / providers drive the most CPU load as RPS climbs?* The `CPU by Endpoint` bar chart correlates directly with the Traffic RPS panel above. |
| **🚨 Errors** | *When the error rate spikes, is it a hot retry loop?* A sudden CPU spike correlated with an error spike means the gateway is hammering a failing provider. |
| **📊 Saturation** | *What is consuming process CPU heading into saturation?* The full flamegraph shows the exact stack frames. Use `$provider` and `$model` dropdowns to isolate. |

Sampling rate: **100 Hz** (every 10 ms). Flush interval: **15 s**. Tags: `service_name`, `environment`, `endpoint`, `method`, `provider`, `model`.

panels.title: 🔥  CPU Flamegraph — Full Process (All Pillars)  |  service_name=gatewayz-backend
panels.description: Process-wide flamegraph for the selected time window. Combines samples from every request. Use this to answer: what function is consuming the most CPU right now? This is the single most powerful root-cause tool for all four golden signal pillars.

panels.title: 📊  CPU Load by Pillar Dimension  |  Endpoint (Traffic) · Provider (Latency) · Model (Saturation)
🚦  Traffic → CPU by Endpoint  (top endpoints driving load)
panels.description: Pyroscope CPU samples accumulated per endpoint. Directly correlates with the Traffic RPS panel — high RPS on an endpoint means high CPU here. An endpoint with disproportionate CPU vs RPS is inefficient.

panels.title: ⚡  Latency → CPU by Provider  (which providers cost the most CPU?)
panels.description: CPU samples per upstream provider. A provider with high CPU samples that also has high P99 latency (see Latency pillar) is the root cause. Cross-reference with the P99 latency panel above.

panels.title: 📊  Saturation → CPU by Model  (which models saturate the process?)
panels.description: CPU samples per model. When process CPU is high (Saturation pillar), this panel identifies whether it is one model type dominating. Useful for detecting runaway streaming requests for large models.

panels.title: 📈  CPU Sample Rate Over Time — by Provider  (correlate spikes with Latency / Error pillars above)
panels.description: Pyroscope CPU sample rate (samples/sec) over time, stacked by provider. A spike here that coincides with a P99 latency spike (Latency pillar) or error rate spike (Errors pillar) confirms the hot provider. Flat line when PYROSCOPE_ENABLED=false.

panels.title: 🔬  CPU Flamegraph — Filtered by $provider / $model  (use dropdowns to zoom into specific calls)
panels.description: Drill-down flamegraph scoped to the selected provider and model. Use the $provider and $model template variables at the top of this dashboard. When a specific provider is causing latency (Latency pillar) or errors (Errors pillar), set $provider to that provider here to see exactly which Python code is running during its calls.

title: GatewayZ — Four Golden Signals (SRE)
